Response: Simbios, NIH National Center for Physics-Based Simulation of Biological Structures at Stanford University; primary developers Michael Sherman and Peter Eastman (Stanford Simbios Center); Scott Delp Neuromuscular Biomechanics Lab (Stanford)

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: High-performance open-source C++ multibody dynamics library for simulating articulated biomechanical and mechanical systems; provides accurate physics simulation of skeletons, robots, and vehicles; serves as the physics engine underlying OpenSim (biomedical simulation software)

Metric: Number of developers (all developers that have contributed at least one commit to the project) (use repo commit logs) (number)
Response: ~40 (GitHub contributors on simbody/simbody; primary authors Michael Sherman and Peter Eastman plus academic community contributions)

Metric: How is the project funded? (unfunded, unclear, funded∗) where ∗ requires a string to say the source of funding
Response: funded (NIH grants supporting the Simbios Center at Stanford; ongoing NIH support through OpenSim/Simbios; Professor Scott Delp's neuromuscular biomechanics research grants)

Metric: Initial release date? (date)
Response: 2011 (first major paper; code roots date to NIH Internal Variable Dynamics Module by Charles Schwieters; moved to GitHub October 2013)

Metric: Last commit date? (date)
Response: 2024-12 (actively maintained; SimTK project page references dates into 2025)

Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Response: yes (designed for high-performance real-time simulation; uses efficient Featherstone O(n) dynamics algorithms; optimized for large-scale biomechanical models with hundreds of bodies)

column4: Simbody originates from NIH/Stanford work on biological simulation. It is the core physics engine for OpenSim, the industry-standard biomechanical simulation software used in clinical gait analysis and prosthetics research. Simbody handles compliant contact, constraint-based simulation, and articulated body inertia efficiently. Has 5,000+ commits. Primary use case is biomedical simulation but also applied to robotics.

column4: Conda install is the simplest path (single command). CMake from source is straightforward for a low-level C++ library. Documentation is clear and well-maintained. Primary audience is biomechanics researchers who may prefer OpenSim's GUI over direct Simbody access.
